---
title: Making R a First-Class Environment for LLMs
subtitle: "mcp-repl: a private, sandboxed REPL for agents"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    controls: true
    progress: true
    width: 1600
    height: 900
---

## R Is Interactive

- Live session state
- Object inspection
- Help and vignettes
- Plots you can iterate on
- Debuggers and readline prompts

::: notes
R is not just a language for running scripts. Effective R work happens
in live sessions: load data, inspect objects, read help, make a plot,
notice what is wrong, and try again. You step into browser, inspect a
frame, and continue. If we want language models to be effective in R,
code execution is not enough. They need interactive affordances.
:::

## Agents Usually Get Shell Commands

```sh
Rscript -e '...'
python -c '...'
```

- Every call starts over
- Loaded data and objects disappear
- Plots and help are awkward
- Terminal sessions add polling and guesswork

::: notes
Most general-purpose coding agents start with shell commands. That is
fine for isolated probes, but it is a poor fit for data work. Loading
data can be expensive, and objects in memory become context for the next
question. A terminal or tmux session preserves state, but leaves the
agent polling text, guessing whether R is idle, and guessing how to
handle prompts, pagers, plots, and debuggers.
:::

## What mcp-repl Gives the Agent

- A private, persistent R or Python session
- In-band help, manuals, and vignettes
- Plot capture through MCP
- Debugger and readline workflows
- Bounded output and transcript bundles
- Interrupt and reset controls

::: notes
mcp-repl is an open-source MCP server that gives an agent a private,
persistent REPL. The agent can keep state across tool calls, read R help
in-band, receive plots as MCP images, drive debugger and readline
workflows, and recover with interrupt and reset controls. The Python
backend follows the same idea, but the key point for useR is R: expose
the interactive R workflow in a form an LLM can use.
:::

## Why the Runtime Is Private

- Built for unattended or lightly supervised work
- No shared human console
- Sandboxed by default
- Narrow MCP interface
- You can launch an agent and come back later

::: notes
This is not a notebook and it is not a human-facing IDE. The session is
private to the agent. That tradeoff is useful for unattended work:
evals, recurring reports, scheduled checks, or investigations where you
may come back hours later. Because the model owns the runtime, the
sandbox matters. By default, network access is disabled, writes are
constrained, and the boundary is enforced with operating-system
primitives rather than prompt instructions.
:::

## What You Can Ask It To Do

- Draft recurring reports from fresh data
- Run data-analysis evals with tools like Inspect
- Explore a dataset and produce reconnaissance
- Reproduce and debug package failures
- Refine plots and tables before human review

::: notes
The workflows are concrete. You can ask an agent to analyze last week's
sales data, find what changed, and draft a report that highlights what
is fresh or concerning. You can give an eval harness a realistic R
runtime for measuring agent capability on data-analysis tasks. Or ask
the agent to explore a dataset, reproduce a failing package example,
inspect live objects, step through browser, and propose a small fix.
:::

## Under the Hood

- R and Python run embedded in the worker
- mcp-repl owns enough of the REPL loop
- No prompt-string polling or fixed sleeps
- Large output stays bounded
- Plots and transcripts remain inspectable

::: notes
The implementation detail that matters is that mcp-repl is not scraping
a terminal. R and Python run embedded in the worker, and mcp-repl owns
enough of the REPL loop to know when the interpreter is ready. That
avoids prompt-string polling and fixed sleeps. It also lets the tool
shape output for models: concise echo, bounded large outputs, image
responses for plots, and structured bundles for oversized results.
:::

## Where Posit Assistant Fits

- mcp-repl: plug-in runtime for agents
- Posit Assistant: integrated human-in-the-loop product
- Same goal: make R better for AI-assisted data work
- Different interface: autonomous runtime vs shared IDE context

::: notes
mcp-repl also shows the limits of a plug-in architecture. It works with
existing agents such as Claude Code and Codex, and it can make those
tools much better at R work. Posit Assistant solves a different problem.
It is integrated into the IDE, so the human and model share project
context. Together, they show two ways to bring R's interactive
affordances to LLMs: autonomous private runtimes and close
collaboration.
:::

## Takeaway

`mcp-repl` makes R and Python more capable environments for agents.

Private runtime. Persistent state. Interactive affordances. Sandboxed
execution.

https://github.com/posit-dev/mcp-repl

::: notes
The takeaway is simple: making LLMs effective in R means more than
giving them a code execution button. They need the interactive
affordances of an R session, shaped for a model rather than a human
console. mcp-repl is a focused open-source tool for that job: a private,
persistent, sandboxed runtime for agents doing real data work.
:::
